_doc: Detailed source evidence (NOT rendered on the blog). The post body shows only summarySources; full cross-validation lives here. One claim = >=2 independent sources (crossChecked) before it may appear in the post.

claims.statement: A cryptographic hash is a one-way function: it maps arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length digest and is computationally infeasible to invert (preimage resistance), so you cannot recover the input from the output.

sources.title: Hash function (Glossary)
sources.quote: one-way: given the output, it should be impractical or impossible to regenerate the original input

sources.title: Cryptographic hash function
sources.quote: a one-directional mathematical operation which is quick to calculate, yet hard to reverse

claims.note: Three independent sources; primary (NIST) + two reputable secondaries.

claims.statement: Encryption is reversible: with the correct key, ciphertext can be decrypted back to plaintext. Its goal is confidentiality, which is the opposite design intent from a one-way hash.

sources.title: Cryptographic hash function (contrast with encryption)
sources.quote: Whereas encryption is reversible (you can decrypt ciphertext back to plaintext using a key), cryptographic hashing is not.

sources.title: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
sources.note: Authenticated encryption modes 'provide guarantees of the integrity and authenticity of the data, as well as confidentiality' — encryption's job is confidentiality.

claims.statement: A bare hash provides no authentication: because no secret is involved, anyone who alters the message can recompute a matching digest. Authentication requires a secret key.

sources.title: RFC 2104 — HMAC: Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication
sources.quote: a mechanism for message authentication using cryptographic hash functions ... requires a cryptographic hash function H and a secret key K

sources.title: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
sources.note: A MAC provides integrity and authenticity (alteration detected + origin verified); a keyless hash does not.

claims.note: Keyed authentication necessity stated by RFC 2104 and corroborated by OWASP.

claims.statement: HMAC = H(K XOR opad, H(K XOR ipad, text)). It requires a shared secret key K plus a hash function H, and provides message authentication: verifying both data integrity and the authenticity of the sender.

sources.title: RFC 2104 — HMAC: Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication
sources.quote: H(K XOR opad, H(K XOR ipad, text))

sources.title: The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC) — FIPS 198-1
sources.note: US federal standard adopting the same HMAC construction.

sources.title: Crypto: createHmac / timingSafeEqual
sources.note: Vendor API implementing HMAC used in the post's code.

claims.note: Formula and properties from RFC 2104; standardized by FIPS 198-1.

claims.statement: The naive MAC Hash(secret || message) is broken by a length-extension attack on Merkle-Damgard hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512): knowing H(x) and len(x), an attacker computes H(x || y) without the secret. SHA-384, SHA-512/256, and SHA-3 are not susceptible, and HMAC is not vulnerable by design.

sources.title: Length extension attack
sources.quote: HMAC ... uses a different construction and so is not vulnerable to length extension attacks

sources.title: Length-extension attacks are still a thing
RFC 2104 (security of nested construction)
sources.note: Nested inner/outer keying is the design that resists this class of attack.

claims.note: Vulnerable-algorithm list and HMAC immunity confirmed across three independent sources.

claims.statement: Encryption alone does not guarantee integrity or authenticity; ciphertext in unauthenticated modes (e.g. AES-CBC) can be modified (bit-flipping). Use authenticated encryption (GCM/CCM) or Encrypt-then-MAC.

sources.title: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
sources.quote: As these do not provide any guarantees about the authenticity of the data, separate authentication should be implemented, such as using the Encrypt-then-MAC technique.

sources.title: Testing for Padding Oracle (WSTG)
sources.note: Demonstrates that unauthenticated CBC ciphertext can be manipulated.

claims.statement: HMAC verification must use a constant-time comparison; ordinary === / string compare returns early on the first mismatching byte and leaks how many leading bytes matched, letting an attacker reconstruct a valid tag byte by byte. Node.js exposes crypto.timingSafeEqual for this.

sources.title: Crypto: crypto.timingSafeEqual(a, b)
sources.note: Compares two buffers in constant time; documented as suitable for comparing HMAC digests.
